content:
Hollis Akins is an undergraduate student currently studying physics at Grinnell College in Grinnell, IA. There he works as mentor at the Data Analysis and Social Inquiry Lab and plays an active role in the student Astronomy Group. His primary research interests are in astrophysics and cosmology, and he is pursuing research opportunities in these fields.He is an avid photographer and enjoys exploring art, design, music, and other creative efforts.
This website is a place to showcase personal projects, artistic endeavors, research, and those places where they all overlap.

summary: Snapshots of the night sky and its many mysteries.
title: Astrophotography

summary: A series of surreal photographs created with a unique analog darkroom process.
title: Perspectives

content: I worked as a Research Assistant at the Guilford College Cline Observatory in the summer of 2018, studying variable stars and constructing a system for automatic data analysis and collection. The project was intended primarily to facilitate future student research using the tools I developed. While the nature of the project did not necessitate a final research paper or presentation, I have compiled a short summary of the work I did and the preliminary results obtained, which can be found here.
summary: Summer research project at the Guilford College Cline Observatory, developing a tool for programatic photometry and variable star studies.
title: Data Pipeline

content: For my final project for an observational astronomy class at Guilford College, I used a method called Lucky Imaging to photograph planets and incoorporate them into artistic photographs. The project was exhibited as a poster presentation at the Guilford Undergraduate Symposium, and turned into an article which was published in the October 2018 edition of The Physics Teacher.
summary: Artistic planetary astrophotography using the lucky imaging technique.
title: Lucky Imaging

content: While not a full-fledged research project, I have used Geographic Information Systems (GIS) software to study the impacts of urban sprawl on developing cities. This was a final project for a GIS class at Guilford College, completed over three weeks in the fall of 2018. The final paper can be found here.
summary: Short GIS analysis of urban sprawl.
title: Urban Sprawl

title: Imaging planets from imaginary worlds
